agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation) with tiered policies and trial management. The rules govern payment validation, renewal retry logic, cancellation policies, and trial abuse prevention.","bounded_context":"Subscription Billing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Subscription","definition":"A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, progressing through states: trial, active, past-due, cancelled."},{"term":"Payment Method","definition":"A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees. Has a validity status (active/failed)."},{"term":"Billing Cycle","definition":"The recurring period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when charges are collected."},{"term":"Renewal","definition":"The automated attempt to charge the payment method and extend the subscription for another billing cycle."},{"term":"Grace Period","definition":"A window of time after payment failure during which the subscription remains active before cancellation is enforced (applies to Premium tier)."},{"term":"Free Trial","definition":"A time-limited subscription that does not charge the customer, used for onboarding and conversion."},{"term":"Premium Tier","definition":"A higher-value subscription level that receives preferential cancellation policies (14-day grace period)."},{"term":"Failed Payment Method","definition":"A payment instrument that has been declined or is otherwise unable to process charges."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription with a known-bad payment method would create a fundamentally corrupt state: an active billing agreement with no ability to collect revenue. This is not a policy preference — it is a precondition for the subscription entity to validly exist in an active state. Violating this produces an immediately inconsistent system where the platform has obligations (service delivery) with no means of fulfillment (payment collection).","testable_assertion":"Given a customer with only failed payment methods, when they attempt to activate a subscription, then the activation MUST be rejected and the subscription MUST NOT transition to active state."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 7-day lead time is a business policy decision balancing retry opportunity against customer experience. A VP could reasonably change this to 5 days or 10 days based on payment processor retry success rate data. The underlying principle (renew early enough to allow retries) is sound, but the specific window is a tunable threshold, not a physical or data-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given an active subscription, when the current date is exactly 7 days before the billing cycle end date, then the system MUST initiate an automatic renewal charge attempt."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 3-strike threshold is a configurable business policy. Management could decide that 5 retries are more customer-friendly, or that 2 retries reduce financial exposure. The count is a policy lever, not a data integrity constraint. However, the principle that SOME finite limit exists before cancellation could be argued as invariant — but the specific number 3 is clearly policy.","testable_assertion":"Given an active subscription with 2 prior failed renewal attempts, when a third renewal attempt fails, then the subscription MUST be automatically transitioned to cancelled state."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state transition integrity constraint. The cancelled state is terminal by design. Allowing reactivation of cancelled subscriptions would break the subscription lifecycle state machine, create audit trail ambiguities (which billing cycle does the reactivated subscription belong to?), and potentially corrupt revenue recognition. This is not about policy preference — it enforces that the subscription entity's state graph has a well-defined terminal node, which is essential for data consistency and financial auditability.","testable_assertion":"Given a subscription in cancelled state, when any attempt is made to transition it to active state, then the system MUST reject the transition and return an error indicating that a new subscription must be created."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 14-day grace period and its applicability to Premium tier are both business policy decisions. The duration could be changed to 21 days, or extended to other tiers, based on churn analysis and customer lifetime value calculations. This is a differentiated service policy, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal, when 14 days have NOT yet elapsed since the first failure, then the subscription MUST remain active and MUST NOT be cancelled. When 14 days have elapsed without a successful payment, then the subscription MUST be automatically cancelled."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a uniqueness constraint that preserves data integrity and prevents trial abuse. One-trial-per-email is not a soft preference — it is a hard constraint that prevents economic exploitation of the platform. Allowing duplicate active trials for the same email would corrupt the business model by enabling infinite free service. This is analogous to a unique constraint in a database: the system is in an invalid state if two active trials exist for the same email.","testable_assertion":"Given a customer email address that already has an active free trial subscription, when an attempt is made to create a second free trial for that same email, then the system MUST reject the creation and the total count of active trials for that email MUST remain exactly one."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer whose only payment method on file has status 'failed'","when":"The customer attempts to activate a new subscription","then":"The system rejects activation with a clear error message and no subscription state change occurs"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An active subscription whose billing cycle ends in exactly 7 days","when":"The system runs its renewal scheduler","then":"A renewal charge attempt is initiated against the customer's payment method"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An active (non-Premium) subscription that has accumulated 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts","when":"The third failure is recorded","then":"The subscription is automatically transitioned to cancelled state and the customer is notified"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A subscription in cancelled state","when":"Any actor (customer, admin, system) attempts to reactivate it","then":"The operation is rejected with an error; the customer is directed to create a new subscription"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal attempt","when":"Fewer than 14 days have elapsed since the first failure","then":"The subscription remains active; no cancellation occurs regardless of retry count within that window"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal","when":"14 days have elapsed since the first failure without a successful payment","then":"The subscription is automatically cancelled"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An email address that already has an active free trial subscription","when":"A request to create a new free trial is submitted for the same email","then":"The request is rejected and the existing trial remains unaffected"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer fixes their payment method between renewal retries","expected_behavior":"The next scheduled retry should succeed, reset the failure count, and the subscription should continue normally without cancellation."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber's payment fails, they downgrade to standard tier during the 14-day grace period","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — does the grace period persist or does the 3-strike rule take effect immediately? Needs product clarification."},{"scenario":"Customer has a cancelled trial and attempts a new trial with the same email","expected_behavior":"Should be allowed — the rule says one ACTIVE trial per email. A cancelled trial does not block a new one."},{"scenario":"Renewal attempt initiated at day 7 but payment processor is down","expected_behavior":"The failed attempt counts as attempt 1; the system should retry per its schedule. The 7-day window provides buffer for exactly this scenario."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber has payment failure on day 13 of grace period, then succeeds on day 14","expected_behavior":"If payment succeeds before the cancellation trigger fires on day 14, the subscription should remain active."},{"scenario":"Customer has multiple email aliases pointing to the same mailbox","expected_behavior":"The uniqueness constraint is on the email string, not the mailbox. alias+1@example.com and alias+2@example.com would be treated as different emails unless the system normalizes them."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"These rules directly protect revenue integrity. BR-1 prevents creating uncollectable subscriptions. BR-3/BR-5 balance revenue recovery (retries) against bad debt exposure. BR-6 prevents trial abuse that erodes conversion economics.","operational":"Auto-renewal at 7 days (BR-2) reduces support ticket volume from surprised customers. The Premium grace period (BR-5) reduces churn for highest-value customers. Terminal cancellation (BR-4) simplifies subscription state management and audit trails.","user":"Customers benefit from retry windows that prevent accidental service disruption due to temporary payment issues. Premium customers get preferential treatment aligned with their higher spend. The no-reactivation rule (BR-4) could frustrate customers who expect to simply 'turn it back on.'","risk":"BR-4 (no reactivation) creates churn risk if the UX for creating a new subscription is cumbersome. BR-6 (trial uniqueness) may be circumvented by email aliases unless addressed. The interaction between BR-3 and BR-5 for tier changes mid-failure is undefined.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers (Standard tier)","impact":"Subject to 3-strike cancellation; need clear communication about retry schedule and impending cancellation."},{"group":"Customers (Premium tier)","impact":"Receive preferential 14-day grace period; higher retention but expectation of continued preferential treatment."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue team","impact":"Rules directly affect revenue recognition timing, bad debt provisioning, and subscription metrics (MRR, churn rate)."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will handle escalations from customers surprised by cancellation, especially around the no-reactivation policy (BR-4)."},{"group":"Product/Growth team","impact":"Trial abuse prevention (BR-6) directly affects conversion funnel metrics and CAC calculations."}]},"confidence":{"level":72,"reasoning":"The spec is relatively clear and internally consistent for a billing domain. Most rules are well-defined with explicit thresholds. Confidence is not higher because several interaction patterns between rules are unspecified (e.g., tier change during grace period, exact retry schedule within the 7-day window, email normalization for trial uniqueness).","high_confidence_areas":["Subscription activation requires valid payment (BR-1)","Cancelled state is terminal (BR-4)","One active trial per email (BR-6)","3-strike cancellation for standard tier (BR-3)"],"low_confidence_areas":["What happens when a Premium subscriber downgrades during the 14-day grace period — which cancellation policy applies?","Exact retry schedule within the 7-day renewal window (how are the 3 attempts spaced?)","Whether email normalization is expected for trial uniqueness (aliases, plus-addressing, case sensitivity)","Whether the 14-day Premium grace period resets on each failed attempt or starts from the first failure"],"assumptions":["The 7-day renewal window and 3-strike rule imply retries are spaced within that 7-day window, though exact spacing is not specified","'Failed payment method' in BR-1 means the method's current status, not historical failures","The 14-day grace period for Premium starts from the first failure, not from each subsequent failure","Email uniqueness for trials is based on exact string match unless normalization is explicitly specified"]},"escalations":[],"rejection_reasons":[]}
